story.headline: Washington outlasts Miami in 35-28 battle
body: Washington found just enough in a 35-28 win over Miami in Week 2 PCFL action, with Graham Mertz earning Player of the Game honors after going for 30 of 53 for 389 yards and 3 TDs.
The Huskies were outgained by Miami 404–330 in total offense, including 93 yards on the ground. Miami kept fighting, managing 404 total yards on the night.
Up next, Washington travels to face Oregon in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Mertz's arm carries Miami, but Washington's ground game proves decisive
body: Graham Mertz had no answer when asked why his 389 passing yards and three touchdowns weren't enough to get Miami over the hump in a 35-28 loss to Washington. The Hurricanes quarterback was sharp and decisive all night, completing 30 of 53 attempts and finding Campen for eight receptions and 89 yards. Mertz's performance was the kind that ordinarily wins games on the road, but it ran headlong into a Washington team that understood what Miami couldn't seem to execute: the value of feeding a back repeatedly until the defense breaks.

Washington's rushing attack accumulated just 93 yards on 11 carries, hardly a dominant showing on paper. But that number masks the real story. The Huskies controlled the clock and the line of scrimmage when it mattered most, allowing their defense to stay fresh and their passing game to operate in rhythm. Graham Brown needed just 13 completions on 17 attempts for 138 yards because Washington never got behind. When the Huskies needed a touchdown in the air, WR James took a slant 103 yards worth of impact, breaking tackles and moving the chains when scores were tight.

Miami's fumble late didn't cost them the game outright, but it crystallized a broader issue: the Hurricanes lived by the air and, ultimately, fell short by it. A team that generates 404 yards of offense and scores 28 points should win more often than not. Yet in a game where one team will take what the defense gives them and the other keeps pressing for more, Washington's restraint and execution in critical moments separated the two.
aiStory.turningPoint: Washington's ability to sustain drives with the ground game despite modest rushing totals kept Miami's talented defense on the field long enough to wear them down and prevented the Hurricanes from capitalizing on their passing efficiency.

unsungHero.name: Clemons
unsungHero.why: Clemons' 12 tackles and disruptive sack set the tone for Washington's defensive resilience without the flashiness of a pick or fumble recovery, keeping Miami's high-powered passing attack consistently off balance.

story.headline: Notre Dame steamrolls Arkansas, 35-10
body: Notre Dame dominated from start to finish in a 35-10 rout over Arkansas in Week 2 PCFL action, with Hollywood Smothers earning Player of the Game honors after going for 62 rushing yards and 2 rushing TDs.
The Fighting Irish outgained Arkansas 478–310 in total offense, including 145 yards on the ground. Arkansas kept fighting, managing 310 total yards on the night.
Up next, Notre Dame hosts Georgia in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Notre Dame's Ground Game Overwhelms Arkansas in Convincing Week 2 Win
body: Notre Dame's offensive identity crystallized Saturday in a 35-10 victory over Arkansas, with Hollywood Smothers muscling through the Razorback defense for two touchdown runs and establishing a physical tone that Arkansas never answered. The Irish rushed for 145 yards on the afternoon, not gaudy by any standard, but deployed with surgical precision to set up manageable third downs and keep the chains moving when the passing game faced coverage adjustments.

Jackson's night for Arkansas told the story of a quarterback fighting an uphill battle. He completed 13 of 30 attempts for 249 yards but managed no touchdowns, and more troubling, his receivers—including Clay's respectable 91-yard haul—operated in isolation rather than as a coordinated attack. The Razorbacks accumulated only 86 rushing yards across the game, forcing Jackson into long-yardage situations and eliminating the play-action looks that could have kept Carr honest.

Notre Dame's Carr was efficient without spectacular, completing 24 of 39 for 357 yards and two touchdowns, the kind of workmanlike performance that reflects offensive patience and balance. Fuller's 92 receiving yards and one touchdown gave the Irish a second receiving threat, but Smothers remained the engine. Arkansas had no answer for his downhill running on early downs, and once the Irish established that tone, the game's direction became inevitable.
aiStory.turningPoint: Smothers' first touchdown run, coming early in the second quarter with Notre Dame leading only 7-3, shifted momentum decisively by validating the Irish's power-running scheme and forcing Arkansas into pass-first desperation.

unsungHero.name: Clay
unsungHero.why: He led Arkansas receivers with 91 yards on only four catches, showing route precision and separation that went wasted in an architecture that couldn't protect Jackson long enough to develop timing.

story.headline: Tennessee outlasts Penn State in 26-20 battle
body: Tennessee found just enough in a 26-20 win over Penn State in Week 2 PCFL action, with George MacIntyre earning Player of the Game honors after going for 21 of 28 for 278 yards and 2 TDs.
The Volunteers outgained Penn State 398–281 in total offense, including 89 yards on the ground. Penn State kept fighting, managing 281 total yards on the night.
Up next, Tennessee travels to face Ohio State in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: MacIntyre's precision guides Tennessee past Penn State in defensive struggle
body: George MacIntyre didn't need volume to control this game. The Tennessee quarterback completed 75 percent of his passes for 278 yards and two touchdowns, operating within a scheme that prioritized efficiency over desperation. Penn State's defense bent repeatedly but couldn't break at the moments that mattered most, allowing the Volunteers to convert third downs when the game hung in balance. Tennessee's offense gained 117 more total yards than Penn State without any explosive rushing attack, a statement about rhythm and execution in the passing game.

Penn State quarterback Sean Myers fought all afternoon against a Tennessee secondary that refused him easy completions downfield. His 22 completions on 39 attempts told the story of a young offense searching for answers. Running back Jenkins provided the closest thing to a consistent ground threat with 58 yards on seven carries, but Penn State's run game never threatened to establish dominance. The Nittany Lions scored one touchdown through the air but couldn't manufacture the second score that would have changed the complexion of the fourth quarter.

The turnover margin, often a deciding factor in close contests, played its role here. Tennessee's lone fumble went uncovered, a small mercy in a game where field position and pacing controlled the outcome. MacIntyre's decision-making—no interceptions despite facing pressure—separated two teams that entered the contest with similar defensive intentions. Penn State had chances to tighten the game late, but the precision that beat them early never left Tennessee's offense.
aiStory.turningPoint: MacIntyre led a methodical scoring drive in the third quarter when Penn State's pass rush couldn't consistently disrupt rhythm, extending Tennessee's lead to two scores and taking the momentum from the Nittany Lions' reach.

unsungHero.name: WR Jackson
unsungHero.why: Four receptions for 91 yards represents the difficult catch chains that kept Tennessee's drives alive; MacIntyre gets credit for throwing them, but Jackson's contested ball work against Penn State coverage was the reason those completions existed.

story.headline: Colorado takes control late, tops Georgia 42-28
body: Colorado asserted control when it mattered in a 42-28 win over Georgia in Week 2 PCFL action, with Roger Anderson earning Player of the Game honors after going for 24 of 38 for 350 yards and 3 TDs.
The Buffaloes outgained Georgia 462–393 in total offense, including 117 yards on the ground. Georgia kept fighting, managing 393 total yards on the night.
Up next, Colorado hosts Boston College in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Anderson's arm overwhelms Georgia defense in Colorado road victory
body: Roger Anderson completed his assignments with clinical precision on Saturday, finishing 24 of 38 for 350 yards and three touchdowns to lead Colorado past Georgia 42-28 in Athens. The Buffaloes quarterback operated with patience in the pocket, letting plays develop downfield rather than forcing early throws, and his receivers—particularly Mecum with 89 yards on just three receptions—made the most of their opportunities in space. Colorado's passing attack simply outpaced Georgia's secondary, which couldn't generate the consistent pressure needed to disrupt timing.

Georgia's Giles matched Anderson's touchdown total with four of his own, but the volume of throws required to keep pace told the story of a team playing from behind. The Bulldogs managed just 142 rushing yards, a troubling indicator that Colorado's front seven controlled the line of scrimmage early and forced Georgia into obvious passing situations. Lloyd's 63 yards on 11 carries suggest the ground game never found rhythm, leaving Giles dependent on establishing rhythm through the air without the luxury of a functional run game.

Colorado's decision-making on offense proved the difference. The Buffaloes fumbled once but protected the football otherwise, while Sweeney's 13 carries and one touchdown demonstrated a commitment to establishing physical presence despite Anderson's aerial dominance. That balance—a willing ground game complementing an efficient pass attack—gave Georgia's defense no comfortable answers. When you can't stop the run and can't pressure the quarterback consistently, the margin between the teams expands quickly.
aiStory.turningPoint: Colorado's ability to sustain drives through Anderson's intermediate passing in the second half prevented Georgia from closing what appeared to be a competitive game early, breaking the Bulldogs' resolve.

unsungHero.name: Mecum
unsungHero.why: Three catches for 89 yards represents elite efficiency that set up scoring opportunities, yet Anderson's overall performance overshadows how cleanly Mecum won leverage downfield and converted his touches into chunk plays.

story.headline: Instant classic: LSU edges Oregon, 27-24
body: LSU found just enough in a 27-24 thriller over Oregon in Week 2 PCFL action, with Garrett Nussmeier earning Player of the Game honors after going for 16 of 29 for 212 yards and 3 TDs.
The Tigers were outgained by Oregon 355–250 in total offense, including 78 yards on the ground. Oregon kept fighting, managing 355 total yards on the night.
Up next, LSU travels to face Michigan in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Nussmeier's precision overcomes Oregon's yardage advantage in thriller
body: LSU's Garrett Nussmeier delivered when it mattered most, completing 16 of 29 passes for 212 yards and three touchdowns to lead the Tigers past Oregon 27-24 in a game that unfolded as a study in efficiency versus volume. Oregon dominated the stat sheet with 355 total yards to LSU's 250, but couldn't convert that advantage into points, largely because the Ducks couldn't sustain drives when it counted. Nussmeier's three touchdown passes, anchored by receiver Cake's seven-catch, 117-yard performance including two scores, gave LSU the margin of victory despite being outgained significantly.

The Oregon passing game looked explosive on paper: Dan Thomas connected for 236 yards and three touchdowns across 42 attempts. But that completion percentage tells the real story. Thomas worked behind a line that allowed Mothudi to rack up three sacks, and while Laquon Bryant's 70-yard receiving day suggested deep shots were working, the Ducks couldn't string together the kind of consistent scoring drives that would have put LSU away. Oregon's ground game, led by Hughes with 49 yards on 11 carries, never threatened, and the burden fell entirely on Thomas's arm.

LSU's defense, anchored by Wood's eight tackles, bent without breaking when it absolutely had to. The Tigers forced no turnovers and gave up no sacks, a stability that allowed Nussmeier to operate with confidence in the pocket. That clean pocket was the difference. Oregon's weapons are real, but LSU's decision-making and execution in the red zone proved superior when the scoreboard mattered most.
aiStory.turningPoint: Cake's second touchdown reception gave LSU a 27-17 lead with enough time remaining for Oregon to threaten but not recover, as the Ducks' next three possessions failed to produce points.

unsungHero.name: Wood
unsungHero.why: Eight tackles anchored a defensive effort that allowed LSU to win despite being outgained by over 100 yards; his presence in the middle kept Oregon's drive-extending plays to a minimum and gave Nussmeier time to operate.

story.headline: Instant classic: Clemson edges Boston College, 21-20
body: Clemson found just enough in a 21-20 thriller over Boston College in Week 2 PCFL action, with James Cortez earning Player of the Game honors after going for 19 of 30 for 252 yards and 1 TD.
The Tigers were outgained by Boston College 405–345 in total offense, including 114 yards on the ground. Boston College kept fighting, managing 405 total yards on the night.
Up next, Clemson hosts Oklahoma in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Clemson's defense holds firm as Cortez survives BC's aerial assault
body: Boston College came to Death Valley with a plan to outwork Clemson through the air, and for most of Saturday night they executed it flawlessly. Leavitt completed 27 of 47 passes for 292 yards and two touchdowns, putting sustained pressure on a Clemson secondary that entered the week searching for answers. The Eagles racked up 405 total yards to Clemson's 345, controlled the line of scrimmage for stretches, and had every right to believe they'd stolen a road win. Yet when the final whistle sounded, they'd left Death Valley with nothing but a one-point defeat.

Clemson's victory lived or died on execution in moments that mattered. James Cortez didn't light up the box score with his 19 completions for 231 yards, but he threaded passes into traffic when it counted most and never panicked when Boston College's front four collapsed the pocket. Adams Jr punched in a touchdown that proved to be the margin. The real difference, though, came from a Clemson defense that bent without breaking, limiting BC's explosive plays despite being gashed on the ground and keeping the Eagles from capitalizing on their yardage advantage.

Boston College's sole fumble came at the worst possible moment, and Clemson's defense cashed it in. That turnover swing, combined with Clemson's ability to score twice in the red zone while Leavitt and the Eagles settled for field position elsewhere, separated a close game that could have gone either way. This wasn't a statement victory for Clemson, but it was the kind of win that keeps a season alive when the other team plays well enough to win.
aiStory.turningPoint: Boston College's fumble in the second half forced Clemson's defense to make a stand at a critical juncture, and the Tigers converted the turnover into a touchdown drive that proved to be the decisive one-point margin.

unsungHero.name: Mickens
unsungHero.why: Eight tackles and a relentless motor kept Boston College's passing attack from establishing rhythm, yet he'll be overshadowed by Cortez's game-winning poise despite anchoring the defensive effort that made the difference.

story.headline: Texas pulls away from Ohio State, 29-15
body: Texas asserted control when it mattered in a 29-15 win over Ohio State in Week 2 PCFL action, with Arch Manning earning Player of the Game honors after going for 18 of 29 for 272 yards and 4 TDs.
The Longhorns were outgained by Ohio State 421–370 in total offense, including 107 yards on the ground. Ohio State kept fighting, managing 421 total yards on the night.
Up next, Texas travels to face UCLA in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Manning's arm lifts Texas past Ohio State in second-half shootout
body: Arch Manning did not need volume to dismantle Ohio State's secondary. The Texas quarterback completed just 18 of 29 passes but turned each completion into currency, finishing with four touchdown passes and 272 yards in a 29-15 win that exposed Ohio State's inability to generate pressure on the perimeter. While Sayin threw for 345 yards and kept his offense moving, he found no end zone and watched his receivers work in isolation against a Texas defense that refused to panic.

The disparity in efficiency tells the story. Manning operated in rhythm, with receiver Anderson hauling in five catches for 84 yards and two scores, exploiting Ohio State's coverage on intermediate routes. Parker's six receptions for 92 yards kept the Buckeyes competitive on the numbers, yet those gains came on broken plays rather than clean reads. Texas rode a rushing attack that managed only 107 yards but made them count, avoiding the kind of stalled possessions that forced Sayin into his one critical mistake—a fumble that never recovered.

Ohio State's defensive line created few obstacles for Manning, and that absence of early pressure allowed the Texas offense to establish its rhythm. By the time the second half arrived, the game had settled into a rhythm favoring Texas's poise. The Buckeyes' running back Donaldson punched in a touchdown, but one score was insufficient when Manning was threading passes into space with such precision. Texas's defense, led by Littleton's six tackles, bent without breaking, surrendering yardage but not points when it mattered most.
aiStory.turningPoint: Anderson's second touchdown reception in the third quarter gave Texas a two-score lead that Ohio State could not overcome, as Sayin's subsequent drives stalled against a Texas secondary playing with clear coverage discipline.

unsungHero.name: WR Anderson
unsungHero.why: Two touchdown receptions were overshadowed by Manning's four-touchdown performance, yet Anderson created the primary receiving threat that dictated Texas's tempo and gave the secondary something it could not ignore.

story.headline: Michigan steamrolls UCLA, 51-28
body: Michigan dominated from start to finish in a 51-28 rout over UCLA in Week 2 PCFL action, with Nico Iamaleava earning Player of the Game honors after going for 30 of 46 for 368 yards and 4 TDs.
The Wolverines outgained UCLA 519–479 in total offense, including 183 yards on the ground. UCLA kept fighting, managing 479 total yards on the night.
Up next, Michigan hosts LSU in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Michigan's ground game overwhelms UCLA in second-half defensive breakdown
body: Michigan's defense suffocated UCLA's rhythm in the second half, and the Wolverines' rushing attack—led by running back Brock's two touchdown performances—proved to be the difference in a 51-28 victory that looked closer on the scoreboard than it felt on the field. UCLA's Nico Iamaleava was brilliant in his own right, completing 30 of 46 passes for 368 yards and four touchdowns, but the Bruins' inability to protect the football and shore up their run defense allowed Michigan to control the line of scrimmage when it mattered most.

The turnover disparity told the story. UCLA coughed it up twice—both fumbles—while Michigan's ball security remained pristine. Those two UCLA miscues didn't just cost the Bruins field position; they disrupted the rhythm of an otherwise explosive passing offense. Iamaleava found receivers with consistency, and Harris's five catches for 86 yards gave UCLA vertical threats, but the offense was perpetually starting behind the chains after self-inflicted mistakes.

Michigan's 183 rushing yards against 111 for UCLA reflected a fundamental imbalance. Browne didn't need to shoulder the load; his 23 completions on 33 attempts for 336 yards and two touchdowns showcased efficiency over volume. The Wolverines' defense, anchored by Blackburn's seven tackles, forced UCLA into obvious passing situations and made the Bruins' high-volume passing attack less effective as the game wore on. For all of Iamaleava's talent on display, Michigan's complementary football in the trenches simply won out.
aiStory.turningPoint: UCLA's second-half fumble in Michigan territory with the score within reach short-circuited any momentum the Bruins had built, allowing Michigan to widen the gap through consecutive scores where Brock punched in both of his touchdowns.

unsungHero.name: Brock
unsungHero.why: Browne gets credit for efficiency, but Brock's two rushing touchdowns and 80 yards on limited carries showed Michigan understood UCLA's defensive vulnerabilities and executed the game plan without fanfare—the definition of complementary football in a blowout win.

story.headline: USC survives Oklahoma by 3 in thriller
body: USC found just enough in a 35-32 thriller over Oklahoma in Week 2 PCFL action, with Arnold Glass earning Player of the Game honors after going for 30 of 46 for 388 yards and 3 TDs.
The Trojans outgained Oklahoma 526–445 in total offense, including 91 yards on the ground. Oklahoma kept fighting, managing 445 total yards on the night.
Up next, USC travels to face Miami in Week 3.

aiStory.headline: Burnett's 201 yards lift USC past Oklahoma in offensive shootout
body: USC escaped Norman with a 35-32 victory in a game that never felt like either offense would leave the field. Oklahoma's Arnold Glass authored one of those performances that should have won a shootout—388 yards, three touchdowns, and the Player of the Game honor—but USC's balanced attack and specifically Wes Burnett's eight-catch, 201-yard afternoon proved just enough to outlast the Sooners' air assault.

The difference in execution came down to yards after contact and staying out of their own way. USC's quarterback Marcus Moss completed fewer passes than Glass and threw the same number of touchdowns, yet the Trojans consistently moved the ball into the red zone and converted. Oklahoma's defense, anchored by Fiddler's seven tackles, never generated the pressure needed to disrupt Moss's timing with Burnett, who turned underneath routes into gain situations that wore down the Oklahoma secondary.

The fumble proved immaterial—both teams coughed it up once—but Oklahoma's inability to manufacture stops in the third and fourth quarters told the real story. USC's ground game was unremarkable on paper, but Myers's eight carries kept the chains moving and the Sooners' pass rush from pinning its ears back. When a team wins at home with a quarterback having that kind of statistical day and still falls short, the culprit usually isn't the arm talent; it's complementary football, and that belonged to USC on Saturday.
aiStory.turningPoint: Burnett's 14-yard catch on third-and-11 late in the third quarter extended USC's go-ahead drive and effectively ended Oklahoma's momentum on a night when Glass needed any help the defense could provide.

unsungHero.name: Wes Burnett
unsungHero.why: Eight receptions for 201 yards won't generate Player of the Game votes when Glass threw for 388, but Burnett's consistency and the way he moved the chains against Oklahoma's secondary in critical moments was the real engine of USC's drive to victory.

weeklyWrap.headline: Colorado's Statement Win Reshapes Early Season Pecking Order
weeklyWrap.subhead: A dominant road upset of Georgia and Texas's dismantling of Ohio State suggest the preseason favorites may not hold their thrones for long.
body: Colorado walked into Athens and didn't just beat Georgia—they beat them convincingly, 42 to 28. That's not a close loss that could've gone either way. That's a team that was supposed to be a national title contender getting outplayed from start to finish. The Buffaloes' presence in the power poll's top five was generous heading into the week. Now it looks prescient. What makes this result stick is that Colorado didn't sneak past Georgia; they imposed their style of play and Georgia had no answer. That's the kind of road win that either launches a season or exposes a team as fundamentally overrated. Colorado's coaching and roster just looked better when it mattered most.

Texas made a similar statement at Ohio State, winning 29 to 15 in a game that never felt close. The Longhorns ranked fourth in the power poll and proved it wasn't charity. But what's notable here is that Ohio State, a program that should never be dismissed, looked pedestrian offensively—just 15 points at home against a team that's still settling into its new conference. If there's a weakness in the Big Ten, Columbus might have just broadcast it to the rest of the country.

The other seismic shift came at Tennessee, which held off Penn State 26 to 20 to remain the power poll's top-ranked team. That's a real win, the kind that builds a resume. Washington's 35 to 28 victory over Miami looked cleaner on the surface, but Tennessee had to grind it out against a Big Ten team in a stadium that wanted them to lose. That's where seasons are actually built. Meanwhile, LSU squeaked past Oregon 27 to 24 in what feels increasingly like a rubber match between two teams that haven't solved their deepest problems yet.

Arkansas and Notre Dame produced the week's most lopsided result, the Irish winning 35 to 10, but that's almost beside the point. The meaningful story is that Colorado and Texas have crashed the party at the top, and neither looks like they're leaving. If the power poll is right and Tennessee stays composed, we're watching an actual open field form at the summit rather than a coronation of preseason favorites. Georgia's loss stings worst because it came at home, and Ohio State's defeat carries long-term implications for how we should value the Big Ten this fall.
